name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمُدَّثِّرُ (١)
```

```english
O you wrapped up in your mantle! (1)
```

```arabic
قُمْ فَأَنذِرْ (٢)
```

```english
Rise up and warn! (2)
```

```arabic
وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ (٣)
```

```english
Magnify your Lord, (3)
```

```arabic
وَثِيَابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ (٤)
```

```english
and purify your clothes, (4)
```

```arabic
وَٱلرُّجْزَ فَٱهْجُرْ (٥)
```

```english
and keep away from all impurity! (5)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَمْنُن تَسْتَكْثِرُ (٦)
```

```english
Do not grant a favour seeking a greater gain, (6)
```

```arabic
وَلِرَبِّكَ فَٱصْبِرْ (٧)
```

```english
and be patient for the sake of your Lord. (7)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah contains the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to warn (الإنذار) within a context that suggests it was among the commands of the early days of the Prophetic mission (البعثة). It then alludes to the greatness of the Noble Quran and the majesty of its status, followed by a severe threat against those who confront it with denial and accusations of sorcery, and a condemnation of those who turn away from its call.

The Surah is Meccan, among the ancient ones revealed at the beginning of the Prophetic mission and the emergence of the call (الدعوة). It has even been said that it was the first Surah revealed of the Quran, although this is contradicted by the very verses of the Surah, which explicitly state that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) had already been reciting the Quran to the people and that they had rejected it, turned away from it, and accused it of being "sorcery handed down."

For this reason, some have inclined toward the view that what was revealed first were the seven verses at the beginning of the Surah. This would imply that the Surah was not revealed all at once. While this is not far-fetched considering the text of the seven verses, it is countered by the context of the beginning of Surah al-Alaq, which clearly appears to be the first part of the Quran to be revealed.

Others have suggested that the Surah was the first to be revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when he was commanded to make the call public after having kept it secret for a period at the start of the mission. Thus, it carries the same meaning as His saying: **فَاصْدَعْ بِما تُؤْمَرُ وَأَعْرِضْ عَنِ الْمُشْرِكِينَ** "So proclaim what you have been commanded, and turn away from the polytheists" (15:94). By this, they sought to reconcile the reports stating it was the first to be revealed, those stating it was revealed after Surah al-Alaq, and those stating that Surah al-Muzzammil and Surah al-Muddaththir were revealed together. However, this view does not exceed the level of mere possibility.

In any case, what is certain is that the Surah is among the earliest Quranic Surahs revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The seven verses we have cited contain the command to warn and the other qualities necessitated by it, which Allah enjoined upon him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ** **O you enwrapped one!** (74:1). *Al-Muddaththir* (المدثر), with a doubled *dal* and *tha*, is originally *al-mutadaththir* (المتدثر), the active participle of *al-tadaththur* (التدثر), meaning to wrap oneself in garments when sleeping.

The meaning is: "O you who are covered in garments for sleep." This is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) while he was in this state. He was addressed by a description derived from his current state as a gesture of intimacy (تأنيسا) and kindness (ملاطفة), similar to His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الْمُزَّمِّلُ** "O you enwrapped one!" (73:1).

It has been said that "enwrapping" (التدثر) refers to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being clothed in Prophethood (النبوة), likening it to a garment with which one is adorned and beautified. It has also been said that it refers to his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) withdrawal and absence from sight; thus, it is an address to him regarding his state in the Cave of Hira. Others have said it refers to rest and leisure, as if it were said to him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "O you who are resting and at leisure, the time for rest has ended, and the time for the burdens of obligations (التكاليف) and the guidance of people has arrived."

While these interpretations are not inherently problematic, the first meaning is what most readily comes to mind.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ** **Arise and warn!** (74:2). It appears that the intent is the command to warn (الإنذار) without regard to who is being warned; thus, the meaning is "perform the act of warning." Some have mentioned that the object of the verb is omitted, and the estimation is "warn your closest kinsfolk," due to its suitability for the beginning of the call, as mentioned in Surah al-Shu'ara.

Others have stated that the omitted object is general, referring to all of humanity, based on His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْناكَ إِلَّا كَافَّةً لِلنَّاسِ** "We did not send you except as a [bearer] of good news and warner to all mankind" (34:28).

Giving good tidings (التبشير) was not mentioned alongside warning (الإنذار)—even though they are like inseparable correlates in the completion of the call—because the Surah is among those revealed at the start of the mission, and warning was the predominant requirement at that time.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ** **and magnify your Lord,** (74:3). That is, attribute majesty (الكبرياء) and greatness (العظمة) to your Lord in belief and practice, in word and deed. This involves hallowing Him, the Exalted, from having anything equal to Him or surpassing Him; nothing shares with Him, overcomes Him, or obstructs Him. No deficiency affects Him, and no description limits Him.

For this reason, it has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that the meaning of *Takbir* (التكبير) is: "Allah is greater than can be described." He, the Exalted, is greater than every description we might attribute to Him, even this very description. This is what accords with Islamic monotheism (التوحيد), which surpasses the concept of monotheism found in other heavenly religions.

What we have mentioned is the difference between the two phrases of glorification—"Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) and "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله). "Glory be to Allah" (سبحان الله) is hallowing Him, the Exalted, from every privative attribute (وصف عدمي) based on deficiency, such as death, incapacity, ignorance, and the like. "Allah is Greater" (الله أكبر) is an absolute hallowing of Him, the Exalted, from every description we might attribute to Him, whether privative or positive (وجودي), even from this very description itself. This is because every concept (مفهوم) is limited in itself and does not extend to other concepts, while He, the Exalted, is not encompassed by any limit (حد). Understand this well.

It has also been said that the intent is the command to say the *Takbir* in prayer (الصلاة).

The expression of Him, the Exalted, as "your Lord" (ربك) is not without an indication that His monotheism (توحيده) was at that time unique to the Prophet.

The author of *al-Kashshaf* said regarding His saying **فَكَبِّرْ**: "The *fa* (ف) entered to convey the meaning of a condition (الشرط), as if it were said: 'Whatever the case may be, do not abandon His magnification.'"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ** **and purify your clothes,** (74:4). It has been said that this is a metaphor (كناية) for the rectification of deeds (إصلاح العمل). This is not without merit, for deeds are like garments for the soul (النفس) in relation to its beliefs; the outward is the title of the inward. In their speech, the rectification of deeds is often metaphorically expressed as the purity of garments.

It has also been said that it is a metaphor for the purification of the soul (تزكية النفس) and hallowing it from sins and acts of disobedience.

Others have said it means shortening the garments, as this is further from impurity (النجاسة); if they were long and trailed on the ground, one could not be sure they would remain pure.

It has also been said that it means purifying one's spouses from disbelief and disobedience, based on His saying: **هُنَّ لِباسٌ لَكُمْ** "They are a garment for you" (2:187).

It has also been said that the speech is to be taken literally, meaning the purification of clothes from impurities for prayer. The most likely view according to this is that His saying **وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ** refers to the *Takbir* of prayer, and the two verses are aimed at legislating the essence of prayer (أصل الصلاة) concurrently with the command to the call.

The objection that these verses were revealed when there was no prayer at all is not valid. While the legislation of the five daily obligatory prayers as they are today occurred on the Night of Ascension (ليلة المعراج)—being ten units (ركعات) in total, then increased by seven—the essence of prayer existed since the beginning of the mission. This is evidenced by its mention in this Surah and in Surahs al-Alaq and al-Muzzammil, and is supported by narrations.

It has also been said that purifying the clothes means adopting noble character (الأخلاق الحميدة) and virtuous dispositions (الملكات الفاضلة).

There are other opinions regarding the meaning of purifying the clothes which we have refrained from citing, as they can be traced back to some of the aforementioned views. The most preferred of the mentioned views are the first and the fifth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ** **and abandon abomination,** (74:5). It has been said that *al-rujz* (الرجز), with either a *damma* or *kasra* on the *ra*, means punishment (العذاب). The intent of abandoning it is to abandon its cause, which is sin and disobedience. Thus, the meaning is: "Abandon sin and disobedience."

It has also been said that *al-rujz* is a name for every foul and loathsome act or character trait. Thus, the command to abandon it is a command to leave everything that Allah dislikes and does not approve of, absolutely. Or, it is a command to specifically leave base and blameworthy character traits, assuming that "purifying the clothes" refers to leaving sins and acts of disobedience.

It has also been said that *al-rujz* refers to idols; thus, it is a command to abandon the worship of idols.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ** **and do not confer a favor to seek more,** (74:6). What the context of the verses provides and what suits the occasion is that "conferring a favor" (المن) refers to spoiling a good deed by mentioning it to the recipient, as in His saying: **لا تُبْطِلُوا صَدَقاتِكُمْ بِالْمَنِّ وَالْأَذى** "do not render your charities void by reproaches and injury" (2:264), and His saying: **يَمُنُّونَ عَلَيْكَ أَنْ أَسْلَمُوا** "They count it as a favor to you that they have embraced Islam" (49:17). The meaning of "seeking more" (الاستكثار) is seeing the thing and counting it as much, not the seeking of abundance.

The meaning is: "Do not count as a favor your compliance with these commands, your undertaking of the warning, your magnification of your Lord, your purification of your clothes, and your abandonment of abomination, while viewing that as much and being impressed by it. For you are but a servant who possesses nothing of yourself except what Allah has given you ownership of and enabled you to do. He is the Owner of what He gave you and the One Powerful over what He enabled you to do. To Him belongs the command, and upon you is compliance."

Others have offered different interpretations of the verse that do not harmonize as well with the context. It has been said the meaning is: "Do not give a gift in order to be given more than it."

It has also been said: "Do not count as a favor what Allah has given you of Prophethood and the Quran toward the people, seeking thereby a great reward."

It has also been said: "Do not count the delivery of the message as a favor upon your community."

It has also been said: "Do not become weak in your work by considering your acts of obedience to be many."

It has also been said: "Do not count your giving as a favor upon the people, viewing it as much."

It has also been said: "If you give a gift, give it for your Lord and be patient until He is the one who rewards you."

It has also been said that it is a prohibition of forbidden usury (الربا), meaning: "Do not give something seeking to be given more than what you gave."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلِرَبِّكَ فَاصْبِرْ** **and be patient for the sake of your Lord.** (74:7). That is, for the sake of your Lord. Patience (الصبر) is absolute, encompassing patience during calamity, patience in obedience, and patience against disobedience. The meaning is: "For the sake of your Lord, be patient with the calamities and harm that afflict you in your undertaking of the warning and your compliance with these commands; be patient in obedience to Allah, and be patient against disobeying Him." This is a comprehensive meaning for the various points mentioned in the exegesis of the verse, such as the view that it is a command for the act itself without regard to its object, or that it is patience with the harm of the polytheists, or patience in performing the obligatory duties, and so on.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: al-Tayalisi, Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, Ibn al-Durays, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Mardawayh, and Ibn al-Anbari in *al-Masahif* narrated from Yahya ibn Abi Kathir who said: "I asked Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman about the first part of the Quran to be revealed. He said: **يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ** (O you enwrapped one!). I said: 'They say it is **اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ** (Read in the name of your Lord who created)?' Abu Salamah said: 'I asked Jabir ibn Abd Allah about that, and I said to him exactly what you said to me.' Jabir said: 'I only tell you what the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) told us.'"

He said: "**جاورت بحراء فلما قضيت جواري نوديت ـ فنظرت عن يميني فلم أر شيئا ـ ونظرت عن شمالي فلم أر شيئا ، ونظرت خلفي فلم أر شيئا ـ فرفعت رأسي فإذا الملك الذي جاءني بحراء ـ جالس على كرسي بين السماء والأرض ـ فجثت منه رعبا فرجعت فقلت : دثروني دثروني ـ فنزلت : يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ ـ إلى قوله ـ وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ**" — "I stayed in seclusion at Hira, and when I finished my seclusion, I was called. I looked to my right and saw nothing; I looked to my left and saw nothing; I looked behind me and saw nothing. Then I raised my head, and there was the angel who had come to me at Hira, sitting on a chair between the heaven and the earth. I was overcome with fear of him, so I returned and said: 'Wrap me up! Wrap me up!' Then there was revealed: 'O you enwrapped one! Arise and warn...' up to His saying '...and abandon abomination.'"

**I say:** This tradition is contradicted by other traditions indicating that Surah *Iqra* was the first part of the Quran to be revealed. This is supported by the context of Surah *Iqra*. Furthermore, his saying "there was the angel who had come to me at Hira" suggests that revelation (الوحي) had descended upon him previously.

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah: "We said: 'O Messenger of Allah, what should we say when we enter the prayer?' So Allah revealed **وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ** (and magnify your Lord), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) commanded us to open the prayer with the *Takbir*."

**I say:** There is something questionable in this narration, for Abu Hurayrah was among those who embraced Islam long after the Migration (الهجرة), while the Surah is among those revealed at the beginning of the mission. Where were Abu Hurayrah or the companions on that day?

In *al-Khisal*, from Amir al-Mu'minin (عليه‌السلام) in the "Hadith of the Four Hundred": "**تشمير الثياب طهور لها قال الله تبارك وتعالى : وَثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ يعني فشمر**" — "Shortening the garments is a purification for them; Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: 'and purify your clothes,' meaning shorten them."

**I say:** There are several reports with this meaning narrated in *al-Kafi* and *al-Majma'* from Abu Ja'far, Abu Abd Allah, and Abu al-Hasan (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim (who authenticated it) and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Jabir who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: **وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ** (and abandon abomination) with a *damma* on the *ra*." He said: "They are the idols."

**I say:** His saying "They are the idols" is from the speech of Jabir or another narrator in the chain.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَمْنُنْ تَسْتَكْثِرُ** (and do not confer a favor to seek more), and in the narration of Abu al-Jarud, he says: "**لا تعط تلتمس أكثر منها**" — "Do not give while seeking more than it."
